message:
fcp-bootstrap/workflow: honor force_overwrite by bypassing partial-state detection

`check_initialization_state(dir, force_overwrite)` walks the
bootstrap working directory for phase-lock markers, partial-genesis
artifacts, and the recovery marker to classify the state as
`Fresh`, `PartialState { phase }`, `CorruptGenesisWithRecovery`, or
one of the other initialization outcomes. The caller uses that
classification to decide between "keep going from the partial state"
and "start over from scratch".

The `force_overwrite` flag was plumbed through the function
signature but never consulted — the partial-state and
corrupt-genesis branches fired even when the caller explicitly
asked for a fresh bootstrap. That defeated the purpose of the flag
(operators running `ft bootstrap --force` after a botched genesis
were still being told to recover).

Fix: before the partial-state branch, check `if force_overwrite { ... return
Ok(InitCheckResult::Fresh); }`. Inside that branch, log
`tracing::warn!(phase = %phase, "Force overwrite enabled, ignoring
partial bootstrap markers")` when a partial-state marker was
observed, so the operator sees which marker was intentionally
overridden.

Two new regression tests:

* `test_check_init_state_force_overwrite_ignores_partial_state_markers`:
  writes a `BootstrapPhase::KeyGeneration` phase-lock into a tempdir,
  calls `check_initialization_state(dir, true)`, and asserts the
  result is `InitCheckResult::Fresh`.
* `test_check_init_state_force_overwrite_ignores_corrupt_genesis_partial_state`:
  writes a phase-lock for `BootstrapPhase::GenesisCreate` plus a
  plainly non-CBOR `genesis.cbor` blob (so the non-force path would
  otherwise return a corrupt-genesis result), then calls
  `check_initialization_state(dir, true)` and again asserts
  `InitCheckResult::Fresh`.

No change to the non-force path or any of the existing test
scenarios.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
